architecture.summary: LiT (Locked-image Tuning) is a contrastive learning method that aligns pre-trained image models with text models for zero-shot transfer. During training, a frozen pre-trained image encoder produces fixed embeddings, while a trainable text encoder learns to map natural language descriptions into the image embedding space via contrastive loss (maximizing similarity of paired image-text embeddings, minimizing non-paired). At test time, class descriptions are embedded with the text model and matched against image embeddings for zero-shot classification or retrieval. The method decouples image representation learning (via supervised or self-supervised pre-training on clean labeled data) from vision-language alignment (via noisier image-text web data).

biblio.title: LiT: Zero-Shot Transfer with Locked-image text Tuning

claims.statement: With the transformer-based pre-trained ViT-g/14 model, the LiT model achieves 85.2% zero-shot transfer accuracy on the ImageNet test set
LiT achieves 82.5% accuracy on the challenging ObjectNet test set, outperforming the previous state-of-the-art method (CLIP) by 10.2%
With LiT the model achieves 81.7% top-1 accuracy on zero-shot ImageNet transfer with only 300M image-text pairs seen, compared to CLIP which required 12.8B image-text pairs to reach 76.2% accuracy
Using only public datasets (YFCC100m-CLIP and CC12M), LiT achieves 75.7% zero-shot transfer on ImageNet, an absolute improvement of 30.9% over the previously reported state-of-the-art result
On the 4 billion image-text pair dataset, the Lu (locked image) setting achieves 70.1% ImageNet accuracy compared to Uu (unlocked pre-trained image) at 57.2% and uu (random image) at 50.6%

Locking the image tower leads to substantially worse contrastive loss on the dataset used for LiT, but substantially better loss on out-of-distribution datasets such as COCO captions, indicating that the locked text model learns to align with a general image representation rather than specializing to the tuning dataset

LiT works reliably with multiple pre-training methods (supervised and unsupervised) and across diverse architectures (ResNet, Vision Transformers and MLP-Mixer) using three different image-text datasets

Training on the full unfiltered YFCC100m dataset with multilingual text improves performance on non-English languages much more than on English, and using a multilingual pre-trained text model (mT5) significantly helps languages that do not use the Latin script
